sections.title: SCOPE
sections.text:
The provisions of these Guidelines apply to Formulated Complementary Foods for Older Infants and Young Children as defined in Section 3.1 below and include but are not limited to porridges containing cereals, ready-to-use products and food-based home fortificants. Micronutrient supplements, processed cereal based foods, and canned baby foods are not covered by these Guidelines.
These Guidelines should be used in accordance with the Global Strategy for Infants and Young Child Feeding and World Health Assembly Resolution WHA54.2 (2001).

path_titles: Formulated Complementary Foods for Older Infants and Young Children
sections.text: means foods that are suitable for use during the complementary feeding period. These foods are specifically formulated with appropriate nutritional quality to provide additional energy and nutrients to complement the family foods derived from the local diet by providing those nutrients which are either lacking or are present in insufficient quantities.

sections.title: All milled cereals suitable for human consumption
path_titles: All milled cereals suitable for human consumption
sections.text: may be used provided that they are processed in such a way as to reduce the fibre content, when necessary, and to decrease and, if possible, to eliminate anti-nutrients such as phytates, tannins or other phenolic materials, lectins, trypsin, and chymotrypsin inhibitors which can lower the protein quality and digestibility, amino acid bioavailability and mineral absorption. The use of appropriate enzymes may be considered to decrease fibre and anti-nutrients, if needed.

sections.title: Oil Seed Flours and Oil Seed Protein Products
path_titles: Oil Seed Flours and Oil Seed Protein Products
sections.text: Flours, protein concentrates and protein isolates of oil seeds are acceptable if manufactured to appropriate specifications which assure sufficient reduction of anti -nutritional factors and undesirable toxic substances such as trypsin and chymotrypsin inhibitors and gossypol. The decision to add oil seeds flour to a formulated complementary food should take into account local conditions and requirements. Such oil seeds may include Soya beans: dehulled flour, (full fat and defatted) protein concentrate, protein isolate Groundnuts: paste, protein isolate Sesame seed: whole ground and defatted flour Cottonseed: defatted flour Sunflower seed: defatted flour, full fat Lower erucic acid rapeseed: full fat flour.

sections.title: Animal Source Foods
sections.text: Animal source foods such as meat, fish, poultry, eggs, milk and milk products are nutrient dense and good sources of high quality proteins and micronutrients and incorporation of these foods or their derived protein concentrates in Formulated Complementary Foods as technologically feasible is encouraged.

sections.title: Fats and Oils
sections.text: Fats and oils can be incorporated in adequate quantities as technologically feasible for the purpose of increasing the energy density of the product. Care must be taken to avoid oxidized fat which will adversely affect nutrition, flavour and shelf life. Such care is important for fat -containing ingredients (e.g., oil seed flours and oil seed protein products, fish meals, and fish protein concentrates) as well as fats and oils. Partially hydrogenated fats (and oils) should not be used in Formulated Complementary Foods.

sections.title: Legumes and pulses must be appropriately processed to reduce, as much as possible, the anti -nutritional factors normally present, such as phytate, lectin s (haemagglutenins), trypsin and chymotrypsin inhibitors.
path_titles: Legumes and pulses must be appropriately processed to reduce, as much as possible, the anti -nutritional factors normally present, such as phytate, lectin s (haemagglutenins), trypsin and chymotrypsin inhibitors.
sections.text: When phytoestrogen containing legumes and pulses such as soya are added as an ingredient, products with low levels of phytoestrogens should be used. Lectins can be reduced by moist heat treatment; Trypsin inhibitor activity may be reduced to acceptable levels by heating to high temperatures or by prolonged boiling. Phytate can be reduced enzymatically or by soaking or fermentation. Phytoestrogens can be reduced by fermentation.

page_spans.text: When phytoestrogen containing legumes and pulses such as soya are added as an ingredient, products with low levels of phytoestrogens should be used. Phytate can be reduced enzymatically or by soaking or fermentation. Phytoestrogens can be reduced by fermentation.

sections.text: The decision to add oil seeds flour to a formulated complementary food should take into account local conditions and requirements. sections.title: Food additives and flavourings
sections.text:
Food additives and flavourings listed in the Standard for Processed Cereal -Based Foods for Infants and Young Children (CODEX STAN 74 -1981) and the Standard for Canned Baby Foods (CODEX STAN 73 -1981) may be used in Formulated Complementary Foods to the maximum limits given in those Standards. Only the food additives referred to in those Standards may be present in the foods covered by these Guidelines, as a result of carry-over from a raw material or other ingredients (including food additives) used to produce the food, subject to the following conditions:
a) The amount of the food additive in the raw materials or other ingredients (including food additives) does not exceed the maximum level specified; and
b) The food into which the food additive is carried over does not contain the food additive in greater quantity than would be introduced by the use of the raw material or ingredients under good manufacturing practice, consistent with the provisions on carry-over in the Preamble of the General Standard for Food Additives (CODEX STAN 192-1995).

sections.title: Dehulling

sections.text: when necessary, pulses, legumes, oilseeds and certain cereals such as oats, barley, sorghum, millet and teff should be dehulled as completely as is feasible to reduce the fibre content to acceptable levels and to decrease, and if possible, to eliminate phytates, tannins and other phenolic materials, trypsin and chymotrypsin inhibitors which can lower the protein digestibility and amino acid bioavailability and mineral absorption.

sections.title: Cereals and pulses can be induced to germinate by soaking or humidifying. It is necessary, however, to ensure that growth of mycotoxin producing microorganisms does not occur. The action of natural amylases contained in the grains results in the predigestion of the starchy portion of the grain (dextrinization) thus reducing the bulk of the food when prepared for feeding and, ultimately, increasing the nutrient density of the food. Sprouting, malting and fermentation can induce hydrolysis of phytates and decrease its inhibitory effect on mineral absorption, and may improve B vitamin content.
path_titles: TECHNOLOGIES FOR AND EFFECTS OF PROCESSING

Cereals and pulses can be induced to germinate by soaking or humidifying. It is necessary, however, to ensure that growth of mycotoxin producing microorganisms does not occur. The action of natural amylases contained in the grains results in the predigestion of the starchy portion of the grain (dextrinization) thus reducing the bulk of the food when prepared for feeding and, ultimately, increasing the nutrient density of the food. Sprouting, malting and fermentation can induce hydrolysis of phytates and decrease its inhibitory effect on mineral absorption, and may improve B vitamin content.

The action of natural amylases contained in the grains results in the predigestion of the starchy portion of the grain (dextrinization) thus reducing the bulk of the food when prepared for feeding and, ultimately, increasing the nutrient density of the food. Sprouting, malting and fermentation can induce hydrolysis of phytates and decrease its inhibitory effect on mineral absorption, and may improve B vitamin content.

sections.title: The mix of milled or ground basic ingredients (cereals, pulses, oilseed flours) may be further processed by extrusion cooking. Extrusion cooking may decrease available L-lysine, sulphur-containing amino acids, L-arginine, L-tryptophan and vitamins. The process should therefore be carefully controlled. The extruded product, after drying if necessary, is milled or ground to the desired particle size.

sections.title: With this process the milled or ground basic ingredients (cereals, pulses, and oilseed flours) can be processed in the presence of water and appropriate enzymes under continuous stirring until the mixture acquires the desired fluidity. In the case of the use of amylase, starch molecules are split into dextrins and reducing sugars. After raising the temperature to inactivate the enzyme, the slurry is dried and comminuted to flour or to small flakes to allow for greater nutrient density.
path_titles: TECHNOLOGIES FOR AND EFFECTS OF PROCESSING

With this process the milled or ground basic ingredients (cereals, pulses, and oilseed flours) can be processed in the presence of water and appropriate enzymes under continuous stirring until the mixture acquires the desired fluidity. In the case of the use of amylase, starch molecules are split into dextrins and reducing sugars. After raising the temperature to inactivate the enzyme, the slurry is dried and comminuted to flour or to small flakes to allow for greater nutrient density.

source.process: WITH THIS PROCESS THE MILLED OR GROUND BASIC INGREDIENTS (CEREALS, PULSES, AND OILSEED FLOURS) CAN BE PROCESSED IN THE PRESENCE OF WATER AND APPROPRIATE ENZYMES UNDER CONTINUOUS STIRRING UNTIL THE MIXTURE ACQUIRES THE DESIRED FLUIDITY. sections.title: Ten to fifty grammes of the Formulated Complementary Food, when prepared according to the instructions, is considered a reasonable quantity which an older infant or young child during the complementary feeding period can ingest easily in one feeding and who may receive two or more feedings per day, depending on age.
sections.text: The range in amount per feeding allows for the various types of Formulated Complementary Foods. The lower part of the range applies to products with higher energy density (e.g. lipid -based products) whereas the upper part of the range would apply to products with lower energy density (e.g. porridges containing cereals).

sections.title: If, for technical reasons, the PDCAAS digestibility of a protein cannot be determined, the protein quality should be measured by biological assays. Alternatively, the protein quality may be calculated from published data on essential amino acid patterns of dietary proteins and their digestibility.
sections.retrieval_text:
Proteins > 6.3.3
number=6.3.3
level=3

source.process: IF, FOR TECHNICAL REASONS, THE PDCAAS DIGESTIBILITY OF A PROTEIN CANNOT BE DETERMINED, THE PROTEIN QUALITY SHOULD BE MEASURED BY BIOLOGICAL ASSAYS. ALTERNATIVELY, THE PROTEIN QUALITY MAY BE CALCULATED FROM PUBLISHED DATA ON ESSENTIAL AMINO ACID PATTERNS OF DIETARY PROTEINS AND THEIR DIGESTIBILITY.
process_candidates: If, for technical reasons, the PDCAAS digestibility of a protein cannot be determined, the protein quality should be measured by biological assays. sections.text: 6.5.1  Starch is likely to be a major constituent of many Formulated Complementary Foods. Any carbohydrate added for sweetness should be used sparingly.

sections.title: Dietary fibres and other non -absorbable carbohydrates are partially fermented by the intestinal flora
path_titles: Dietary fibres and other non -absorbable carbohydrates are partially fermented by the intestinal flora
sections.text: Increasing the intake of dietary fibres increases stool bulk, may cause flatulence and decrease appetite. Fibre load also can reduce the energy density of Formulated Complementary Foods. They also may affect the efficiency of absorption of important nutrients from diets with marginal nutrient contents. The dietary fibre content of the Formulated Complementary Food should therefore be reduced to a level not exceeding 5 g per 100 g on a dry weight basis.

sections.title: Setting levels for the addition of vitamins and minerals
path_titles: Setting levels for the addition of vitamins and minerals
sections.text: 6.6.1.1 The decision to add vitamins and minerals to a Formulated Complementary Food should take into account local conditions including the nutrient contribution to the diet from local foods, vitamins and minerals provided by national programs, food processing technologies applied and the nutritional status of the target population as well as the requirements stipulated by national legislation and the General Principles for the Addition of Essential Nutrients to Foods (CAC/GL 9-1987). 6.6.1.2 If the dietary intake data for the target population are available, they can be used to determine appropriate levels for the addition of vitamins and/or minerals to ensure a low prevalence of either inadequate or excessive nutrient intakes using available assessment or monitoring tools. 6.6.1.3 If the dietary intake data for the target population is not available, the vitamins and minerals listed in the Table in the Annex to these Guidelines can be used as a reference for the selection of particular vitamins and minerals and their amounts for addition to a Formulated Complementary Food.

sections.title: Selecting vitamins and/or minerals for nutrient addition
path_titles: Selecting vitamins and/or minerals for nutrient addition
sections.text: 6.6.3.1 When establishing the specifications for the premix of vitamin compounds and mineral salts, the vitamin and mineral content and presence of antinutritive substances in the other ingredients used in the formulation of the food should be taken into account. 6.6.3.2 Vitamins and/or minerals should be selected from the Advisory Lists of Nutrient Compounds for Use in Foods for Special Dietary Uses intended for Infants and Young Children (CAC/GL 10 -1979) those authorised for cereal-based foods and canned baby foods. 6.6.3.3 The choice of a vitamin and/or mineral compound should take into account its relative bioavailability within the food vehicle, the effect on the sensory properties of the food vehicle and its stability in the packaged food vehicle under normal storage conditions. The General Principles for the Addition of Essential Nutrients to Foods (CAC/GL 9-1987) provides specific guidelines in this area.

sections.title: Pesticides Residues
sections.text: The products should be prepared with special care under good manufacturing practices, so that residues of those pesticides which may be required in the production, storage or processing of the raw materials or the finished food ingredients do not remain, or, if technically unavoidable, are reduced to the maximum extent possible. These measures should take into account the specific nature of the products concerned and the specific population group for which they are intended.

sections.title: LABELLING
sections.text: 10.1  It is recommended that the labelling of Formulated Complementary Foods for Older Infants and Young Children be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of and Claims for Prepackaged Foods for Special Dietary Uses  (CODEX STAN 146 -1985), the Guidelines for Use of Nutrition and Health Claims  (CAC/GL 23-1997) and the Guidelines on Nutrition Labelling (CAC/GL 2-1985). 10.2  The following mandatory provisions should apply: 10.2.1 The Name of the Food The name of the food to be declared on the label shall indicate that the food is a Formulated Complementary Food for Older Infants and Young Children. The appropriate designation indicating the true nature of the food should be in accordance with national legislation. The major sources of protein and the age from which the product is recommended for use shall appear in close proximity to the name of the food. 10.2.2 List of Ingredients The list of ingredients shall be declared in accordance with Section 4 .2 of the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CODEX STAN 1-1985). 10.2.3 Declaration of Nutritive Value The declaration of energy and nutrients on the label or in labelling shall contain the following information expressed per 100 grammes of  the Formulated Complementary Food as sold or otherwise distributed as well as per feeding of the food ready for consumption: (a) energy value, expressed in kilocalories and kilojoules; (b) the amounts of protein, carbohydrates and fat, expressed in grammes; (c) in addition to any other nutritional information required by national legislation, the total quantity per feeding of the Formulated Complementary Food ready for consumption  of each vitamin and mineral added in accordance with Section 6.6, expressed in metric units.

page_spans.text: 10.1  It is recommended that the labelling of Formulated Complementary Foods for Older Infants and Young Children be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of and Claims for Prepackaged Foods for Special Dietary Uses  (CODEX STAN 146 -1985), the Guidelines for Use of Nutrition and Health Claims  (CAC/GL 23-1997) and the Guidelines on Nutrition Labelling (CAC/GL 2-1985). 10.2  The following mandatory provisions should apply: 10.2.1 The Name of the Food The name of the food to be declared on the label shall indicate that the food is a Formulated Complementary Food for Older Infants and Young Children. The appropriate designation indicating the true nature of the food should be in accordance with national legislation. The major sources of protein and the age from which the product is recommended for use shall appear in close proximity to the name of the food. 10.2.2 List of Ingredients The list of ingredients shall be declared in accordance with Section 4 .2 of the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CODEX STAN 1-1985). 10.2.3 Declaration of Nutritive Value The declaration of energy and nutrients on the label or in labelling shall contain the following information expressed per 100 grammes of  the Formulated Complementary Food as sold or otherwise distributed as well as per feeding of the food ready for consumption: (a) energy value, expressed in kilocalories and kilojoules; (b) the amounts of protein, carbohydrates and fat, expressed in grammes; (c) in addition to any other nutritional information required by national legislation, the total quantity per feeding of the Formulated Complementary Food ready for consumption  of each vitamin and mineral added in accordance with Section 6.6, expressed in metric units.

sections.title: Other Contaminants
sections.text: The product should not contain contaminants or other undesira ble substances (e.g. biologically active substances) in amounts which may represent a hazard to the health of older infants and young children. The product covered by the provisions of these Guidelines shall comply with those maximum residue limits and maximum levels established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.

sections.title: HYGIENE
sections.text: 8.1 It is recommended that the products covered by the provisions of these Guidelines be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles o f Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969) and other relevant Codex texts such as Codes of Hygienic Practice and Codes of Practice. The product should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CAC/GL 21-1997). 8.2 The ingredients and final product should be prepared, packed and held under sanitary conditions and should comply with relevant Codex texts16.

sections.title: PACKAGING
sections.text: 9.1  It is recommended that Formulated Complementary Foods for Older Infants and Young Children be packed in containers which will safeguard the hygienic and other qualities of the food. 9.2  The containers, including packaging material, shall be made  only of materials which are safe and suitable for their intended uses. Where the Codex Alimentarius Commission has established a standard for any such substance used as packaging material, that standard shall apply.

sections.title: Instructions for use
sections.text: The label should indicate clearly from which age the product is recommended for use. This age shall not be less than six months for any product. In addition, the label shall include a statement indicating that the decision when precisely to introduce formulated complementary feeding, including any exception to six months of age, should be made in consultation with a health worker, based on the individual infant's specific growth and development needs. Additional requirements in this respect may be made in accordance with the legislation of the country in which the product is sold.

sections.title: ANNEX TABLE
sections.text: The reference INL 98 values listed in the Table provide a guide for selection and amounts of vitamins and minerals to be added to a Formulated Complementary Food. The suggested total quantity of each of these vitamins and/or minerals contained in a daily ration of the Formulated Complementary Food is at least 50% of INL98. VITAMINS AND MINERALS REFERENCE NUTRIENT INTAKE or Individual Nutrient Levels98 (INL98)17 Vitamin A µg retinol equivalent 400 Vitamin D18 µg 5 Vitamin E mg (α-Tocopherol) 5 Vitamin C mg 30 Thiamine mg 0.5 Riboflavin mg 0.5 Niacin mg NE 6 Vitamin B6 mg 0.5 Folate µg DFE 150 Vitamin B12 µg 0.9 Biotin µg 8 Pantothenic acid mg 2 Vitamin K µg 15 Calcium mg 500 Iron mg19 11.6, 5.8, 3.9 Zinc mg20 8.3, 4.1, 2.4 Iodine µg 90 Copper mg 21 0.34 Selenium µg 17 Magnesium mg 60 Manganese mg 21 1.2 Phosphorus mg 21 460 17 Reference nutrient intake or INL 98 from FAO/ WHO Vitamins and Mineral requirements in Human Nutrition.2 nd Edition. FAO/WHO 2004 (for all nutrients except copper, manganese and phosphorus) 18 Vitamin D should be added if there is inadequate exposure to sunlight 19 Iron values are given for 5%, 10 % and 15% dietary iron bioavailability 20 Zinc values are given for low, medium and high dietary zinc bioavailability 21 Values are Dietary Reference Intakes. Institute of Medicine, 1997/2001 (Source for Copper, Manganese and Phosphorus).

sentences.original: The decision to add oil seeds flour to a formulated complementary food should take into account local conditions and requirements.

classification.sentence: Such oil seeds may include Soya beans: dehulled flour, (full fat and defatted) protein concentrate, protein isolate Groundnuts: paste, protein isolate Sesame seed: whole ground and defatted flour Cottonseed: defatted flour Sunflower seed: defatted flour, full fat Lower erucic acid rapeseed: full fat flour.
classification.function: to list examples of oil seeds
classification.pattern: [Such oil seeds] may include [list of examples].

oil_seeds_examples: Soya beans: dehulled flour, (full fat and defatted) protein concentrate, protein isolate
Groundnuts: paste, protein isolate
Sesame seed: whole ground and defatted flour
Cottonseed: defatted flour
Sunflower seed: defatted flour, full fat
Lower erucic acid rapeseed: full fat flour

classification.justification: Sentence provides a list of examples; no normative modality.

source.subject: INGREDIENT LISTING
subject_candidates: FLOURS, PROTEIN CONCENTRATES AND PROTEIN ISOLATES OF OIL SEEDS ARE ACCEPTABLE IF MANUFACTURED TO APPROPRIATE SPECIFICATIONS4,5,6,7 WHICH ASSURE SUFFICIENT REDUCTION OF ANTI -NUTRITIONAL FACTORS AND UNDESIRABLE TOXIC SUBSTANCES SUCH AS TRYPSIN AND CHYMOTRYPSIN INHIBITORS AND GOSSYPOL.
source.process: FLOURS, PROTEIN CONCENTRATES AND PROTEIN ISOLATES OF OIL SEEDS ARE ACCEPTABLE IF MANUFACTURED TO APPROPRIATE SPECIFICATIONS4,5,6,7 WHICH ASSURE SUFFICIENT REDUCTION OF ANTI -NUTRITIONAL FACTORS AND UNDESIRABLE TOXIC SUBSTANCES SUCH AS TRYPSIN AND CHYMOTRYPSIN INHIBITORS AND GOSSYPOL.
process_candidates: Flours, protein concentrates and protein isolates of oil seeds are acceptable if manufactured to appropriate specifications4,5,6,7 which assure sufficient reduction of anti -nutritional factors and undesirable toxic substances such as trypsin and chymotrypsin inhibitors and gossypol.

source.section: 4.1.3.1 - Flours, protein concentrates and protein isolates of oil seeds are acceptable if manufactured to appropriate specifications4,5,6,7 which assure sufficient reduction of anti -nutritional factors and undesirable toxic substances such as trypsin and chymotrypsin inhibitors and gossypol.

section_path_titles: Oil Seed Flours and Oil Seed Protein Products

Flours, protein concentrates and protein isolates of oil seeds are acceptable if manufactured to appropriate specifications4,5,6,7 which assure sufficient reduction of anti -nutritional factors and undesirable toxic substances such as trypsin and chymotrypsin inhibitors and gossypol.

sentences.original: Such oil seeds may include Soya beans: dehulled flour, (full fat and defatted) protein concentrate, protein isolate Groundnuts: paste, protein isolate Sesame seed: whole ground and defatted flour Cottonseed: defatted flour Sunflower seed: defatted flour, full fat Lower erucic acid rapeseed: full fat flour.

classification.sentence: Such oil seeds may include Soya beans: dehulled flour, (full fat and defatted) protein concentrate, protein isolate Groundnuts: paste, protein isolate Sesame seed: whole ground and defatted flour Cottonseed: defatted flour Sunflower seed: defatted flour, full fat Lower erucic acid rapeseed: full fat flour.
classification.function: to list examples of oil seeds
classification.pattern: [Such oil seeds] may include [list of examples].

oil_seeds_examples: Soya beans: dehulled flour, (full fat and defatted) protein concentrate, protein isolate
Groundnuts: paste, protein isolate
Sesame seed: whole ground and defatted flour
Cottonseed: defatted flour
Sunflower seed: defatted flour, full fat
Lower erucic acid rapeseed: full fat flour

classification.justification: Sentence provides a list of examples; no normative modality.

sentences.original: Food additives and flavourings listed in the Standard for Processed Cereal -Based Foods for Infants and Young Children (CODEX STAN 74 -1981) and the Standard for Canned Baby Foods (CODEX STAN 73 -1981) may be used in Formulated Complementary Foods to the maximum limits given in those Standards.

classification.sentence:
Only the food additives referred to in those Standards may be present in the foods covered by these Guidelines, as a result of carry-over from a raw material or other ingredients (including food additives) used to produce the food, subject to the following conditions:
a) The amount of the food additive in the raw materials or other ingredients (including food additives) does not exceed the maximum level specified; and
b) The food into which the food additive is carried over does not contain the food additive in greater quantity than would be introduced by the use of the raw material or ingredients under good manufacturing practice, consistent with the provisions on carry-over in the Preamble of the General Standard for Food Additives (CODEX STAN 192-1995).
classification.function: Specifying conditions under which food additives may be present due to carry-over
classification.pattern: Only [Additives] may be present as carry-over subject to conditions: a) [Condition a]; b) [Condition b]

components.Additives: food additives referred to in those Standards
components.Condition a: amount in raw materials or other ingredients does not exceed maximum level specified
components.Condition b: food additive in food does not exceed quantity introduced by raw materials under GMP

classification.justification: Complex conditional permission with enumerated conditions, structure adapted from typical conditional permission templates.

source.subject: EFFECT/OUTCOME DESCRIPTION
subject_candidates: CEREALS AND PULSES CAN BE INDUCED TO GERMINATE BY SOAKING OR HUMIDIFYING. IT IS NECESSARY, HOWEVER, TO ENSURE THAT GROWTH OF MYCOTOXIN PRODUCING MICROORGANISMS DOES NOT OCCUR. THE ACTION OF NATURAL AMYLASES CONTAINED IN THE GRAINS RESULTS IN THE PREDIGESTION OF THE STARCHY PORTION OF THE GRAIN (DEXTRINIZATION) THUS REDUCING THE BULK OF THE FOOD WHEN PREPARED FOR FEEDING AND, ULTIMATELY, INCREASING THE NUTRIENT DENSITY OF THE FOOD. SPROUTING, MALTING AND FERMENTATION CAN INDUCE HYDROLYSIS OF PHYTATES AND DECREASE ITS INHIBITORY EFFECT ON MINERAL ABSORPTION, AND MAY IMPROVE B VITAMIN CONTENT.
source.process: CEREALS AND PULSES CAN BE INDUCED TO GERMINATE BY SOAKING OR HUMIDIFYING. IT IS NECESSARY, HOWEVER, TO ENSURE THAT GROWTH OF MYCOTOXIN PRODUCING MICROORGANISMS DOES NOT OCCUR. THE ACTION OF NATURAL AMYLASES CONTAINED IN THE GRAINS RESULTS IN THE PREDIGESTION OF THE STARCHY PORTION OF THE GRAIN (DEXTRINIZATION) THUS REDUCING THE BULK OF THE FOOD WHEN PREPARED FOR FEEDING AND, ULTIMATELY, INCREASING THE NUTRIENT DENSITY OF THE FOOD. SPROUTING, MALTING AND FERMENTATION CAN INDUCE HYDROLYSIS OF PHYTATES AND DECREASE ITS INHIBITORY EFFECT ON MINERAL ABSORPTION, AND MAY IMPROVE B VITAMIN CONTENT.
process_candidates: Cereals and pulses can be induced to germinate by soaking or humidifying. It is necessary, however, to ensure that growth of mycotoxin producing microorganisms does not occur. The action of natural amylases contained in the grains results in the predigestion of the starchy portion of the grain (dextrinization) thus reducing the bulk of the food when prepared for feeding and, ultimately, increasing the nutrient density of the food. Sprouting, malting and fermentation can induce hydrolysis of phytates and decrease its inhibitory effect on mineral absorption, and may improve B vitamin content.
TECHNOLOGIES FOR AND EFFECTS OF PROCESSING

THE ACTION OF NATURAL AMYLASES CONTAINED IN THE GRAINS RESULTS IN THE PREDIGESTION OF THE STARCHY PORTION OF THE GRAIN (DEXTRINIZATION) THUS REDUCING THE BULK OF THE FOOD WHEN PREPARED FOR FEEDING AND, ULTIMATELY, INCREASING THE NUTRIENT DENSITY OF THE FOOD. SPROUTING, MALTING AND FERMENTATION CAN INDUCE HYDROLYSIS OF PHYTATES AND DECREASE ITS INHIBITORY EFFECT ON MINERAL ABSORPTION, AND MAY IMPROVE B VITAMIN CONTENT.
reference.process: CEREALS AND PULSES CAN BE INDUCED TO GERMINATE BY SOAKING OR HUMIDIFYING. IT IS NECESSARY, HOWEVER, TO ENSURE THAT GROWTH OF MYCOTOXIN PRODUCING MICROORGANISMS DOES NOT OCCUR. THE ACTION OF NATURAL AMYLASES CONTAINED IN THE GRAINS RESULTS IN THE PREDIGESTION OF THE STARCHY PORTION OF THE GRAIN (DEXTRINIZATION) THUS REDUCING THE BULK OF THE FOOD WHEN PREPARED FOR FEEDING AND, ULTIMATELY, INCREASING THE NUTRIENT DENSITY OF THE FOOD. SPROUTING, MALTING AND FERMENTATION CAN INDUCE HYDROLYSIS OF PHYTATES AND DECREASE ITS INHIBITORY EFFECT ON MINERAL ABSORPTION, AND MAY IMPROVE B VITAMIN CONTENT.
process_candidates: Cereals and pulses can be induced to germinate by soaking or humidifying. It is necessary, however, to ensure that growth of mycotoxin producing microorganisms does not occur. The action of natural amylases contained in the grains results in the predigestion of the starchy portion of the grain (dextrinization) thus reducing the bulk of the food when prepared for feeding and, ultimately, increasing the nutrient density of the food. Sprouting, malting and fermentation can induce hydrolysis of phytates and decrease its inhibitory effect on mineral absorption, and may improve B vitamin content.
TECHNOLOGIES FOR AND EFFECTS OF PROCESSING

source.subject: EXTRUDED PRODUCT
subject_candidates: THE MIX OF MILLED OR GROUND BASIC INGREDIENTS (CEREALS, PULSES, OILSEED FLOURS) MAY BE FURTHER PROCESSED BY EXTRUSION COOKING. EXTRUSION COOKING MAY DECREASE AVAILABLE L-LYSINE, SULPHUR-CONTAINING AMINO ACIDS, L-ARGININE, L-TRYPTOPHAN AND VITAMINS. THE PROCESS SHOULD THEREFORE BE CAREFULLY CONTROLLED. THE EXTRUDED PRODUCT, AFTER DRYING IF NECESSARY, IS MILLED OR GROUND TO THE DESIRED PARTICLE SIZE.
source.process: THE MIX OF MILLED OR GROUND BASIC INGREDIENTS (CEREALS, PULSES, OILSEED FLOURS) MAY BE FURTHER PROCESSED BY EXTRUSION COOKING. EXTRUSION COOKING MAY DECREASE AVAILABLE L-LYSINE, SULPHUR-CONTAINING AMINO ACIDS, L-ARGININE, L-TRYPTOPHAN AND VITAMINS. THE PROCESS SHOULD THEREFORE BE CAREFULLY CONTROLLED. THE EXTRUDED PRODUCT, AFTER DRYING IF NECESSARY, IS MILLED OR GROUND TO THE DESIRED PARTICLE SIZE.
process_candidates: The mix of milled or ground basic ingredients (cereals, pulses, oilseed flours) may be further processed by extrusion cooking. Extrusion cooking may decrease available L-lysine, sulphur-containing amino acids, L-arginine, L-tryptophan and vitamins. The process should therefore be carefully controlled. The extruded product, after drying if necessary, is milled or ground to the desired particle size.
TECHNOLOGIES FOR AND EFFECTS OF PROCESSING

Extrusion cooking may decrease available L-lysine, sulphur-containing amino acids, L-arginine, L-tryptophan and vitamins. The process should therefore be carefully controlled. The extruded product, after drying if necessary, is milled or ground to the desired particle size.

The mix of milled or ground basic ingredients (cereals, pulses, oilseed flours) may be further processed by extrusion cooking. Extrusion cooking may decrease available L-lysine, sulphur-containing amino acids, L-arginine, L-tryptophan and vitamins. The process should therefore be carefully controlled. The extruded product, after drying if necessary, is milled or ground to the desired particle size.

source.subject: TEMPERATURE
subject_candidates: WITH THIS PROCESS THE MILLED OR GROUND BASIC INGREDIENTS (CEREALS, PULSES, AND OILSEED FLOURS) CAN BE PROCESSED IN THE PRESENCE OF WATER AND APPROPRIATE ENZYMES UNDER CONTINUOUS STIRRING UNTIL THE MIXTURE ACQUIRES THE DESIRED FLUIDITY. IN THE CASE OF THE USE OF AMYLASE, STARCH MOLECULES ARE SPLIT INTO DEXTRINS AND REDUCING SUGARS. AFTER RAISING THE TEMPERATURE TO INACTIVATE THE ENZYME, THE SLURRY IS DRIED AND COMMINUTED TO FLOUR OR TO SMALL FLAKES TO ALLOW FOR GREATER NUTRIENT DENSITY.
source.process: WITH THIS PROCESS THE MILLED OR GROUND BASIC INGREDIENTS (CEREALS, PULSES, AND OILSEED FLOURS) CAN BE PROCESSED IN THE PRESENCE OF WATER AND APPROPRIATE ENZYMES UNDER CONTINUOUS STIRRING UNTIL THE MIXTURE ACQUIRES THE DESIRED FLUIDITY. IN THE CASE OF THE USE OF AMYLASE, STARCH MOLECULES ARE SPLIT INTO DEXTRINS AND REDUCING SUGARS. AFTER RAISING THE TEMPERATURE TO INACTIVATE THE ENZYME, THE SLURRY IS DRIED AND COMMINUTED TO FLOUR OR TO SMALL FLAKES TO ALLOW FOR GREATER NUTRIENT DENSITY.
process_candidates: With this process the milled or ground basic ingredients (cereals, pulses, and oilseed flours) can be processed in the presence of water and appropriate enzymes under continuous stirring until the mixture acquires the desired fluidity. In the case of the use of amylase, starch molecules are split into dextrins and reducing sugars. After raising the temperature to inactivate the enzyme, the slurry is dried and comminuted to flour or to small flakes to allow for greater nutrient density.
TECHNOLOGIES FOR AND EFFECTS OF PROCESSING

section_path_titles: TECHNOLOGIES FOR AND EFFECTS OF PROCESSING

With this process the milled or ground basic ingredients (cereals, pulses, and oilseed flours) can be processed in the presence of water and appropriate enzymes under continuous stirring until the mixture acquires the desired fluidity. In the case of the use of amylase, starch molecules are split into dextrins and reducing sugars. After raising the temperature to inactivate the enzyme, the slurry is dried and comminuted to flour or to small flakes to allow for greater nutrient density.

reference.subject: TEMPERATURE
subject_candidates: WITH THIS PROCESS THE MILLED OR GROUND BASIC INGREDIENTS (CEREALS, PULSES, AND OILSEED FLOURS) CAN BE PROCESSED IN THE PRESENCE OF WATER AND APPROPRIATE ENZYMES UNDER CONTINUOUS STIRRING UNTIL THE MIXTURE ACQUIRES THE DESIRED FLUIDITY. IN THE CASE OF THE USE OF AMYLASE, STARCH MOLECULES ARE SPLIT INTO DEXTRINS AND REDUCING SUGARS. AFTER RAISING THE TEMPERATURE TO INACTIVATE THE ENZYME, THE SLURRY IS DRIED AND COMMINUTED TO FLOUR OR TO SMALL FLAKES TO ALLOW FOR GREATER NUTRIENT DENSITY.
reference.process: WITH THIS PROCESS THE MILLED OR GROUND BASIC INGREDIENTS (CEREALS, PULSES, AND OILSEED FLOURS) CAN BE PROCESSED IN THE PRESENCE OF WATER AND APPROPRIATE ENZYMES UNDER CONTINUOUS STIRRING UNTIL THE MIXTURE ACQUIRES THE DESIRED FLUIDITY. IN THE CASE OF THE USE OF AMYLASE, STARCH MOLECULES ARE SPLIT INTO DEXTRINS AND REDUCING SUGARS. AFTER RAISING THE TEMPERATURE TO INACTIVATE THE ENZYME, THE SLURRY IS DRIED AND COMMINUTED TO FLOUR OR TO SMALL FLAKES TO ALLOW FOR GREATER NUTRIENT DENSITY.
process_candidates: With this process the milled or ground basic ingredients (cereals, pulses, and oilseed flours) can be processed in the presence of water and appropriate enzymes under continuous stirring until the mixture acquires the desired fluidity. In the case of the use of amylase, starch molecules are split into dextrins and reducing sugars. After raising the temperature to inactivate the enzyme, the slurry is dried and comminuted to flour or to small flakes to allow for greater nutrient density.

classification.sentence: The lower part of the range applies to products with higher energy density (e.g. lipid -based products) whereas the upper part of the range would apply to products with lower energy density (e.g. porridges containing cereals).

classification.justification: Explains range application; no obligation or permission; no known template match.

source.subject: RANGE APPLICATION EXPLANATION
subject_candidates: TEN TO FIFTY GRAMMES OF THE FORMULATED COMPLEMENTARY FOOD, WHEN PREPARED ACCORDING TO THE INSTRUCTIONS, IS CONSIDERED A REASONABLE QUANTITY WHICH AN OLDER INFANT OR YOUNG CHILD DURING THE COMPLEMENTARY FEEDING PERIOD CAN INGEST EASILY IN ONE FEEDING AND WHO MAY RECEIVE TWO OR MORE FEEDINGS PER DAY, DEPENDING ON AGE.
source.process: TEN TO FIFTY GRAMMES OF THE FORMULATED COMPLEMENTARY FOOD, WHEN PREPARED ACCORDING TO THE INSTRUCTIONS, IS CONSIDERED A REASONABLE QUANTITY WHICH AN OLDER INFANT OR YOUNG CHILD DURING THE COMPLEMENTARY FEEDING PERIOD CAN INGEST EASILY IN ONE FEEDING AND WHO MAY RECEIVE TWO OR MORE FEEDINGS PER DAY, DEPENDING ON AGE.
process_candidates: Ten to fifty grammes of the Formulated Complementary Food, when prepared according to the instructions, is considered a reasonable quantity which an older infant or young child during the complementary feeding period can ingest easily in one feeding and who may receive two or more feedings per day, depending on age.

sentences.original: The decision to add vitamins and minerals to a Formulated Complementary Food should take into account local conditions including the nutrient contribution to the diet from local foods, vitamins and minerals provided by national programs, food processing technologies applied and the nutritional status of the target population as well as the requirements stipulated by national legislation and the General Principles for the Addition of Essential Nutrients to Foods (CAC/GL 9-1987).

classification.sentence: If the dietary intake data for the target population is not available, the vitamins and minerals listed in the Table in the Annex to these Guidelines can be used as a reference for the selection of particular vitamins and minerals and their amounts for addition to a Formulated Complementary Food.

sentences.original: 10.1  It is recommended that the labelling of Formulated Complementary Foods for Older Infants and Young Children be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of and Claims for Prepackaged Foods for Special Dietary Uses  (CODEX STAN 146 -1985), the Guidelines for Use of Nutrition and Health Claims  (CAC/GL 23-1997) and the Guidelines on Nutrition Labelling (CAC/GL 2-1985).

classification.sentence: The declaration of energy and nutrients on the label or in labelling shall contain the following information expressed per 100 grammes of  the Formulated Complementary Food as sold or otherwise distributed as well as per feeding of the food ready for consumption: (a) energy value, expressed in kilocalories and kilojoules; (b) the amounts of protein, carbohydrates and fat, expressed in grammes; (c) in addition to any other nutritional information required by national legislation, the total quantity per feeding of the Formulated Complementary Food ready for consumption  of each vitamin and mineral added in accordance with Section 6.6, expressed in metric units.
classification.function: Mandate declaration of energy and nutrients
classification.pattern: The declaration shall contain specified nutritional information per 100g and per feeding

components.declaration_subject: The declaration of energy and nutrients on the label or in labelling
components.requirement: shall contain the following information expressed per 100 grammes ... as well as per feeding ...: (a) energy value ...; (b) amounts of protein, carbohydrates and fat ...; (c) total quantity per feeding of each vitamin and mineral added ...

classification.justification: Complex obligation listing required nutritional info. No known template match.

sentences.original: In addition, the label shall include a statement indicating that the decision when precisely to introduce formulated complementary feeding, including any exception to six months of age, should be made in consultation with a health worker, based on the individual infant's specific growth and development needs.

classification.justification: Use of 'may be made' qualifies possibility of additional requirements tied to national legislation, indicating permission modality.

pages.title: GUIDELINES ON FORMULATED COMPLEMENTARY FOODS FOR OLDER INFANTS AND YOUNG CHILDREN
pages.summary: Página inicial com título do documento e cabeçalho identificador. Sem seções ou texto interno relevante para estruturação na página.

Página com hierarquia clara de seções numeradas. Nível 1 para títulos 1 a 4 e níveis subsequentes para seções numeradas compostas. Texto interno associado corretamente a cada nó, sem cabeçalhos ou rodapés repetitivos. Não há continuidade entre páginas neste conteúdo.

Página com várias subseções numeradas do nível 2 e 3 sob o capítulo 4.1. Texto explicativo detalhado está corretamente associado a cada seção numerada. O cabeçalho contém código do documento e número da página. Não há título principal detectado na página. Não há indicações de continuidade entre páginas para essas seções.

Esta página contém duas principais seções numeradas no nível 1: 4.2 'Other Ingredients' e 5 'TECHNOLOGIES FOR AND EFFECTS OF PROCESSING'. A seção 4.2 possui duas subseções (4.2.1 e 4.2.2) com seus textos explicativos associados. A seção 5 é estruturada com várias subseções e sub-subseções numeradas (5.1 a 5.3, e dentro de 5.1 e 5.2 múltiplos níveis). O texto interno está limpo, sem cabeçalhos ou rodapés repetidos. A seção 5 é continuidade da página anterior, indicada em is_continuation_from_previous_page, sem continuação para a próxima página. O cabeçalho da página é 'CAC/GL 8-1991  4'. Não há título principal do documento detectado nesta página.

A página 5 do documento contém principalmente as seções 5 e 6, com suas respectivas subseções numeradas hierarquicamente. A seção 5 continua da página anterior (5.3.2 e seguintes). A estrutura segue a numeração para definir níveis hierárquicos de 1 a 4, conforme as regras. O texto interno está adequadamente associado a suas seções, e o cabeçalho da página está presente. Não há título principal detectado. Os nós de seção que aparecem só com títulos e poucas ou nenhuma linha na página foram mantidos com texto vazio, dado o foco na estrutura para RAG.

Página 6 do documento contém subseções 6.1.3 (continuação da página anterior) e as seções 6.2, 6.3 e 6.4 com suas respectivas subseções numeradas hierarquicamente. O cabeçalho da página está presente. Não há título principal do documento visível. Todo o conteúdo está organizado para uso em RAG com títulos e textos associados corretamente, e sem inclusão de cabeçalhos ou rodapés na descrição dos textos.

Página contém seções e subseções numeradas hierarquicamente das seções 6.5 a 7.1, com títulos e textos identificados corretamente para uso em RAG. O cabeçalho está presente, sem título principal visível. Não há continuidade de seções das páginas anteriores nem continuidade para as páginas seguintes.

Página apresenta seções principais numeradas 7.2 e 8 a 10, sem subseções detalhadas identificadas independentemente. Cada seção contém seu texto interno associado corretamente, sem continuidade detectada de ou para outras páginas. Cabeçalho identificado. Estrutura clara para uso em RAG.

A página contém duas seções principais: 10.2.4 Instructions for use, com vários subseções detalhadas e texto associado; e 11 Additional Requirements como novo nível 1. A seção 10.2.4 é longa e continua para a próxima página. Estrutura adequada para recuperação baseada em atributos.

A página contém um único título estrutural significativo 'ANNEX TABLE' no nível 1, seguido pelo texto que detalha vitaminas e minerais e suas referências micro-nutricionais. Não há indicações claras de continuidade com outras páginas.
